info.name: U.S. Election 2020
info.description:
## Overview 

This collection provides a number of requests to demonstrate the metadata and data that is available for the data products that are a part of the catalog, U.S. Election 2020, through the RDS API.

## Collection Structure

The collection is organized by RDS data product, there will be a folder for every data product that is a part of the U.S. Election 2020 catalog. These data product folders will contain example requests to query for metadata, record level data, and aggregate level data. 

### Visualizations

Each request also has an associated visualization component that presents the returned data in a stylized format. To use this feature, import this collection into your workspace by clicking the orange "Run in Postman" button in the top right corner of the screen. You will then need to send the request, and toggle over to the "Visualizer" tab in the response pane.

## Creator

The [Rich Data Services (RDS)](https://www.richdataservices.com) API was written by [Metadata Technology North America (MTNA)](https://mtna.us?utm_source=postman&utm_medium=postmanlabs) and is designed to provide data and metadata together in a unified API. MTNA is a business located in Knoxville, TN that provides high-level expertise around the technical management of statistical and scientific data. MTNA has worked with statistical agencies around the globe, creating solutions around comprehensive metadata management, data collection and ingestion, and data dissemination.

## Resources

This collection serves as a quick start guide for querying U.S. Election 2020 data using RDS. To see the full list of available APIs view the [full RDS documentation](https://documenter.getpostman.com/view/2220438/SzS5umZ3?version=latest#0e79e455-0ffc-4566-ab19-d9ecbf8b217f). For more information about the tool in general and to see a list of example applications using RDS, visit the [RDS website](http://www.richdataservices.com?utm_source=postman&utm_medium=postmanlabs). For other postman collections by MTNA, visit the [MTNA Postman page](https://explore.postman.com/3pbKKoXC7kjtrk).

### RDS Open Source SDKs

Want to integrate RDS into your current project? MTNA has open source SDKs to help you get started.
* [RDS Javascript](https://github.com/mtna/rds-js)  
* [RDS Python](https://github.com/mtna/rds-python)  
* [RDS R](https://github.com/mtna/rds-r) 

### Support

Have questions? 
* Join our [slack channel](https://forms.gle/qzvwQE8nsJGvK1TN9) to interact with our developers and others in the RDS community.
* Browse the [knowledge base](https://mtnaus.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/RDSHD/overview) to find answers.
* Submit a ticket at our [service desk](https://mtnaus.atlassian.net/servicedesk/customer/portal/9).

This should include descriptive properties of each variable including its name, label, description, question text (if captured as part of a survey / questionnare) and any associated classification references. This is not meant to provide a detailed view of any particular variable, but a general overview of the variables that are available as part of the product.

query.description: We will use the MTNA format for our response, this will include the variable metadata in the response. In addition, when combined with the inject parameter the records will contain code objects which will allow us to toggle between the code name, value, or both.

Including the inject flag will specify that the raw values should be replaced with their codes if there any associated with that value. In the MTNA format that we are using this will result in the code object being returned. In simpler formats the code name will be returned in place of the code value.
This will limit the number of rows returned.
This will limit the number of columns returned.

item.description:
## Overview Polling data on the approval of Trump's handling of the COVID-19 pandemic. Each record measures the approval and disapproval ratings of the handling of a certain topic by the population type and party of respondents. ## Citation COVID Approval Polls data by FiveThirtyEight, licensed under CC BY 4.0 ## Provenance

This data product is created through cleaning and simple transformations applied to the COVID Approval Polls CSV provided by FiveThirtyEight through their github repository. This data is pulled daily.

Data Curation and Transformation

The following data curation and transformation steps have been performed on the source data file.

  • Many variables have been renamed to be more descriptive and unified by topic.
  • The date_start and date_end variables have been reformatted to be ISO 8601 dates.

For more in-depth information about the data curation process or how this data product's variables were mapped from the source file, please visit this data product's github repository.

## RDS User Interfaces Want to play with the data before diving into the API? Try out some of our data exploration tools that were built entirely off the API to see what it's capable of. * The data and metadata can be explored using the [RDS Explorer](https://public.richdataservices.com/rds/explorer/explore/us_election_2020/538_covid_approval_polls/data). * The data can be tabulated using the [RDS TabEngine](https://public.richdataservices.com/rds/tabengine/tabulation/us_election_2020/538_covid_approval_polls/custom-tables).

item.description:
## Overview Polling data on the approval of Trump's handling of the COVID-19 pandemic with adjustments applied using FiveThirtyEight's poll-averaging algorithm. Each record measures the approval and disapproval (adjusted and unadjusted) ratings for a specific poll by the population type and party of respondents. ## Citation COVID Approval Polls - Adjusted data by FiveThirtyEight, licensed under CC BY 4.0 ## Provenance

This data product is created through cleaning and simple transformations applied to the COVID Approval Polls - Adjusted CSV provided by FiveThirtyEight through their github repository. This data is pulled daily.

Data Curation and Transformation

The following data curation and transformation steps have been performed on the source data file.

  • Many variables have been renamed to be more descriptive and unified by topic.
  • The date_modeled, date_start, and date_end variables have been reformatted to be ISO 8601 dates.

For more in-depth information about the data curation process or how this data product's variables were mapped from the source file, please visit this data product's github repository.

## RDS User Interfaces Want to play with the data before diving into the API? Try out some of our data exploration tools that were built entirely off the API to see what it's capable of. * The data and metadata can be explored using the [RDS Explorer](https://public.richdataservices.com/rds/explorer/explore/us_election_2020/538_covid_approval_polls_adjusted/data). * The data can be tabulated using the [RDS TabEngine](https://public.richdataservices.com/rds/tabengine/tabulation/us_election_2020/538_covid_approval_polls_adjusted/custom-tables).

This visualization looks at the approval of Trump's handling of the COVID-19 pandemic. The percent of respondents that approve or disapproved is displayed on the chart below by date and party affiliation to show the progression of the respondents approval or disapproval over time.
Approval Rating of Trump's Handling of COVID-19 Pandemic

query.description: Our dimensions here are date_modeled and party. Dimensions specify how the measures will be grouped when they are returned. In this case, we will have a record with all of our measures per party, per date.

We will be measuring the percentages of approval and disapproval of Trump's handling of the COVID-19 pandemic. We are summing these values because this is a time series, the default measure would be the count of occurances which will not mean anything. This will be the sum of the approval and disapproval percentage by date and party (our dimensions).
We are ordering the results by date_modeled from the earliest date to the latest and then by party.
We are using the AmCharts format because the visualization uses AmCharts. AmCharts json is pretty similar to a lot of other charting libraries and may be useful even if you are not using AmCharts.
By setting inject to true, we tell RDS that we want the state's name instead of its code value in the results.

query.description: We will use the MTNA format for our response, this will include the variable metadata in the response. In addition, when combined with the inject parameter the records will contain code objects which will allow us to toggle between the code name, value, or both.

Including the inject flag will specify that the raw values should be replaced with their codes if there any associated with that value. In the MTNA format that we are using this will result in the code object being returned. In simpler formats the code name will be returned in place of the code value.

item.description:
## Overview Polling data on the concern of economical impact and infections brought on by the COVID-19 pandemic. Each record measures the degree of concern about particular aspects of the pandemic by the population type and party of respondents. ## Citation COVID Concern Polls data by FiveThirtyEight, licensed under CC BY 4.0 ## Provenance

This data product is created through cleaning and simple transformations applied to the COVID Approval Polls CSV provided by FiveThirtyEight through their github repository. This data is pulled daily.

Data Curation and Transformation

The following data curation and transformation steps have been performed on the source data file.

  • Variables have been renamed to be more descriptive.
  • The date_start and date_end variables have been reformatted to be ISO 8601 dates.
  • The poll_subject variable has been coded.

For more in-depth information about the data curation process or how this data product's variables were mapped from the source file, please visit this data product's github repository.

## RDS User Interfaces Want to play with the data before diving into the API? Try out some of our data exploration tools that were built entirely off the API to see what it's capable of. * The data and metadata can be explored using the [RDS Explorer](https://public.richdataservices.com/rds/explorer/explore/us_election_2020/538_covid_concern_polls/data). * The data can be tabulated using the [RDS TabEngine](https://public.richdataservices.com/rds/tabengine/tabulation/us_election_2020/538_covid_concern_polls/custom-tables).

item.description:
## Overview Polling data on the concern of economical impact and infections brought on by the COVID-19 pandemic with adjustments applied using FiveThirtyEight's poll-averaging algorithm. Each record measures the degree of concern (adjusted and unadjusted) about particular aspects of the pandemic by the population type and party of respondents. ## Citation COVID Concern Polls - Adjusted data by FiveThirtyEight, licensed under CC BY 4.0 ## Provenance

This data product is created through cleaning and simple transformations applied to the COVID Concern Polls - Adjusted CSV provided by FiveThirtyEight through their github repository. This data is pulled daily.

Data Curation and Transformation

The following data curation and transformation steps have been performed on the source data file.

  • Many variables have been renamed to be more descriptive and unified by topic.
  • The date_modeled, date_start, and date_end variables have been reformatted to be ISO 8601 dates.
  • The poll_subject variable has been coded.

For more in-depth information about the data curation process or how this data product's variables were mapped from the source file, please visit this data product's github repository.

## RDS User Interfaces Want to play with the data before diving into the API? query.description: We will use the MTNA format for our response, this will include the variable metadata in the response. In addition, when combined with the inject parameter the records will contain code objects which will allow us to toggle between the code name, value, or both.

Including the inject flag will specify that the raw values should be replaced with their codes if there any associated with that value. In the MTNA format that we are using this will result in the code object being returned. In simpler formats the code name will be returned in place of the code value.

item.name: Concern for the Effects of COVID-19

exec: // get the data set from the postman response

This visualization displays the concern respondents have around the effects of COVID-19 on the economy and their concern of being infected. The percent of people concerned is displayed on the chart below by date to show the progression of the respondents concern over time.
// Create the series and add to charts
function createSeries(chart, field, name, valueAxis, dateAxis) {
// Pass the response body parsed as JSON as `data`

query.description: Our dimensions here are date_modeled and poll_subject. Dimensions specify how the measures will be grouped when they are returned. In this case, we will have a record with all of our measures per subject, per date.

We are measuring the percentage of concern that the respondents have in relation to the subject. We are summing these values because this is a time series, the default measure would be the count of occurances which will not mean anything. This will be the sum of the percentage of concern by date and subject (our dimensions).
We are ordering the results by date_modeled from the earliest date to the latest and then by poll_subject.
We are using the AmCharts format because the visualization uses AmCharts. AmCharts json is pretty similar to a lot of other charting libraries and may be useful even if you are not using AmCharts.
By setting inject to true, we tell RDS that we want the code names instead of code values in the results.

request.description: This query returns the concern that respondents have around the effects of COVID-19 on the economy and their concern of being infected. Specifically, this query will return the percent of people (very, somewhat, not very, not at all) concerned on a specific date by the subject (economy / infection).

item.description:
## Overview Toplines of polling data on the concern of economical impact and infections brought on by the COVID-19 pandemic. Each record measures the daily average level of conern by the party of respondents. ## Citation COVID Approval Toplines data by FiveThirtyEight, licensed under CC BY 4.0 ## Provenance

This data product is created through cleaning and simple transformations applied to the COVID Concern Toplines CSV provided by FiveThirtyEight through their github repository. This data is pulled daily.

Data Curation and Transformation

The following data curation and transformation steps have been performed on the source data file.

  • Variables have been renamed to be more descriptive.
  • The date_modeled variable has been reformatted to be an ISO 8601 date.
  • The party variable has been coded.

For more in-depth information about the data curation process or how this data product's variables were mapped from the source file, please visit this data product's github repository.

## RDS User Interfaces Want to play with the data before diving into the API? Try out some of our data exploration tools that were built entirely off the API to see what it's capable of. * The data and metadata can be explored using the [RDS Explorer](https://public.richdataservices.com/rds/explorer/explore/us_election_2020/538_covid_concern_toplines/data). * The data can be tabulated using the [RDS TabEngine](https://public.richdataservices.com/rds/tabengine/tabulation/us_election_2020/538_covid_concern_toplines/custom-tables).

item.name: 538 Economic Index 2020

request.description: This request returns the data product from the RDS API. It contains descriptive properties such as the data product name, label, description, a citation of the source of the data, provenance information, when the product was last updated, and any additional notes that may be needed to further understand the data or how to use it.

response.name: Data Product Overview
response.body:
{ "cacheLastCleared" : "2020-09-03T04:45:06.665Z", "cached" : true, "citation" : "Economic Index data by dmil at FiveThirtyEight, licensed under CC BY 4.0", "description" : "Economic index data. Each record measures an economic indicator, its current z-score, and projected z-score on election day.", "documentation" : "For more information on the indicators in the data, see this post. The economic indexes were collected from the Federal Reserve Bank Of St. Louis and the stock prices data from Yahoo Finance.", "id" : "538_economic_index", "isPrivate" : false, "lastUpdate" : "2020-08-28T12:54:03.3Z", "name" : "538 Economic Index 2020", "note" : "Time Series

This data product is a time series, as such, the date_modeled should be included in any tabulations performed on it. In addition, tabulations should be created using the sum of a measure rather than the count or percentage of records.

", "provenance" : "

This data product is created through cleaning and simple transformations applied to the Economic Index CSV provided by FiveThirtyEight through their github repository. This data is pulled daily.

Data Curation and Transformation

The following data curation and transformation steps have been performed on the source data file.

  • Many variables have been renamed to be more descriptive and unified by topic.
  • The date_modeled variable has been reformatted to be an ISO 8601 date.
  • The model variable has been coded.
  • The indicator_category variable has been coded.
  • The variables have been reorganized to have the modelling and simulation variables together at the end of the record.

For more in-depth information about the data curation process or how this data product's variables were mapped from the source file, please visit this data product's github repository.

This should include descriptive properties of each variable including its name, label, description, question text (if captured as part of a survey / questionnare) and any associated classification references. This is not meant to provide a detailed view of any particular variable, but a general overview of the variables that are available as part of the product.

item.description:
## Overview Economic index data. Each record measures an economic indicator, its current z-score, and projected z-score on election day. ## Citation Economic Index data by dmil at FiveThirtyEight, licensed under CC BY 4.0 ## Provenance

This data product is created through cleaning and simple transformations applied to the Economic Index CSV provided by FiveThirtyEight through their github repository. This data is pulled daily.

Data Curation and Transformation

The following data curation and transformation steps have been performed on the source data file.

  • Many variables have been renamed to be more descriptive and unified by topic.
  • The date_modeled variable has been reformatted to be an ISO 8601 date.
  • The model variable has been coded.
  • The indicator_category variable has been coded.
  • The variables have been reorganized to have the modelling and simulation variables together at the end of the record.

For more in-depth information about the data curation process or how this data product's variables were mapped from the source file, please visit this data product's github repository.

## RDS User Interfaces Want to play with the data before diving into the API? Try out some of our data exploration tools that were built entirely off the API to see what it's capable of. * The data and metadata can be explored using the [RDS Explorer](https://public.richdataservices.com/rds/explorer/explore/us_election_2020/538_economic_index/data). * The data can be tabulated using the [RDS TabEngine](https://public.richdataservices.com/rds/tabengine/tabulation/us_election_2020/538_economic_index/custom-tables).

query.description: We will use the MTNA format for our response, this will include the variable metadata in the response. In addition, when combined with the inject parameter the records will contain code objects which will allow us to toggle between the code name, value, or both.

Including the inject flag will specify that the raw values should be replaced with their codes if there any associated with that value. In the MTNA format that we are using this will result in the code object being returned. In simpler formats the code name will be returned in place of the code value.
This will limit the number of rows returned.

item.description:
## Overview National topline data for the U.S. 2020 Presidential elections. Each record measures the chances of winning the popular and Electoral College votes, the forecasted number of Electoral College votes, and forecasted national voteshare per candidate on a particular date that simulations were run. ## Citation Presidential National Toplines 2020 data by dmil at FiveThirtyEight, licensed under CC BY 4.0 ## Provenance

This data product is created through cleaning and simple transformations applied to the Presidential National Toplines 2020 CSV provided by FiveThirtyEight through their github repository. This data is pulled daily.

Data Curation and Transformation

The following data curation and transformation steps have been performed on the source data file.

  • Many variables have been renamed to be more descriptive and unified by topic.
  • The date_modeled variable has been reformatted to be an ISO 8601 date.
  • The model variable has been coded.
  • The variables have been reorganized to have the modelling and simulation variables together at the end of the record.

For more in-depth information about the data curation process or how this data product's variables were mapped from the source file, please visit this data product's github repository.

## RDS User Interfaces Want to play with the data before diving into the API? Try out some of our data exploration tools that were built entirely off the API to see what it's capable of. * The data and metadata can be explored using the [RDS Explorer](https://public.richdataservices.com/rds/explorer/explore/us_election_2020/538_presidential_national_toplines_2020/data). * The data can be tabulated using the [RDS TabEngine](https://public.richdataservices.com/rds/tabengine/tabulation/us_election_2020/538_presidential_national_toplines_2020/custom-tables).

## Overview Scenario analysis data for the U.S. 2020 Presidential elections. Each record measures the chances of a different scenario occurring on a particular date that simulations were run. ## Citation Presidential Scenario Analysis 2020 data by dmil at FiveThirtyEight, licensed under CC BY 4.0 ## Provenance

This data product is created through cleaning and simple transformations applied to the Presidential Scenario Analysis 2020 CSV provided by FiveThirtyEight through their github repository. This data is pulled daily.

Data Curation and Transformation

The following data curation and transformation steps have been performed on the source data file.

  • Many variables have been renamed to be more descriptive and unified by topic.
  • The date_modeled variable has been reformatted to be an ISO 8601 date.
  • The model variable has been coded.
  • The variables have been reorganized to have the modelling and simulation variables together at the end of the record.

For more in-depth information about the data curation process or how this data product's variables were mapped from the source file, please visit this data product's github repository.

## RDS User Interfaces Want to play with the data before diving into the API? Try out some of our data exploration tools that were built entirely off the API to see what it's capable of. * The data and metadata can be explored using the [RDS Explorer](https://public.richdataservices.com/rds/explorer/explore/us_election_2020/538_presidential_scenario_analysis_2020/data). * The data can be tabulated using the [RDS TabEngine](https://public.richdataservices.com/rds/tabengine/tabulation/us_election_2020/538_presidential_scenario_analysis_2020/custom-tables).

This data product is created through cleaning and simple transformations applied to the Presidential State Toplines 2020 CSV provided by FiveThirtyEight through their github repository. This data is pulled daily.

Data Curation and Transformation

The following data curation and transformation steps have been performed on the source data file.

  • Many variables have been renamed to be more descriptive and unified by topic.
  • The date_modeled variable has been reformatted to be an ISO 8601 date.
  • The model variable has been coded.
  • The state variable has been replaced with us_state_fips which has FIPS codes instead of state names.
  • There were several records whose states were congressional districts, these have been dropped.
  • The variables have be reorganized to have variables about the chance to win the Electoral College based on a state victory (and vice versa) grouped near the chances of state victory.
  • The variables have been reorganized to have the modelling and simulation variables together at the end of the record.

For more in-depth information about the data curation process or how this data product's variables were mapped from the source file, please visit this data product's github repository.

query.description: We will use the MTNA format for our response, this will include the variable metadata in the response. In addition, when combined with the inject parameter the records will contain code objects which will allow us to toggle between the code name, value, or both.

Including the inject flag will specify that the raw values should be replaced with their codes if there any associated with that value. In the MTNA format that we are using this will result in the code object being returned. In simpler formats the code name will be returned in place of the code value.
This will limit the number of rows returned.

item.name: Battleground State Vote Share and Chance of Winning

exec: // get the data set from the postman response

This visualization provides a look at the 2020 U.S. Presidential election. The charts below display the incumbent and challengers estimated percentage of vote share and chance of winning in battleground states.
var incumbentVoteShare = createSeries(voteSharechart, "voteshare_incumbent", "Vote Share Incumbent", voteShareValueAxis, voteShareDateAxis, "#FF0000");
var challengerVoteShare = createSeries(voteSharechart, "voteshare_challenger", "Vote Share Challenger", voteShareValueAxis, voteShareDateAxis, "#0015BC");
var incumbentWinState = createSeries(winStatechart, "win_state_incumbent", "Win State Incumbent", winStateValueAxis, winStateDateAxis, "#FF0000");
var challengerWinState = createSeries(winStatechart, "win_state_challenger", "Win State Challenger", winStateValueAxis, winStateDateAxis, "#0015BC");
function createSeries(chart, field, name, valueAxis, dateAxis, color) {
// Pass the response body parsed as JSON as `data`

url.raw: https://public.richdataservices.com/rds/api/query/us_election_2020/538_presidential_state_toplines_2020/tabulate?dims=date_modeled,us_state_fips&measure=voteshare_incumbent:SUM(voteshare_incumbent),voteshare_challenger:SUM(voteshare_challenger),win_state_incumbent:SUM(win_state_incumbent),win_state_challenger:SUM(win_state_challenger)&where=(us_state_fips=04 OR us_state_fips=13 OR us_state_fips=27 OR us_state_fips=26 OR us_state_fips=12 OR us_state_fips=19 OR us_state_fips=37 OR us_state_fips=39 OR us_state_fips=48 OR us_state_fips=55 OR us_state_fips=42 OR us_state_fips=33)&orderby=date_modeled ASC,us_state_fips ASC&format=amcharts&inject=true

query.description: Our dimensions here are date_modeled and us_state_fips. Dimensions specify how the measures will be grouped when they are returned. In this case, we will have a record with all of our measures per state, per date.

We will be measuring the voteshare and win percentage for the incumbent and challenger. We are summing these values because this is a time series, the default measure would be the count of occurances which will not mean anything. This will be the sum of the voteshare and win percentage by date and state (our dimensions).

query.value: (us_state_fips=04 OR us_state_fips=13 OR us_state_fips=27 OR us_state_fips=26 OR us_state_fips=12 OR us_state_fips=19 OR us_state_fips=37 OR us_state_fips=39 OR us_state_fips=48 OR us_state_fips=55 OR us_state_fips=42 OR us_state_fips=33)
query.description: The where clause here is allowing us to pick out the battleground states rather than all the states. The where clause must use the code value (the FIPS code) rather than the state name.

We are ordering the results by date_modeled from the earliest date to the latest and then by state. The states are coded so the order is by the state code (not name) but in this case it works out alphabetically as well.
We are using the AmCharts format because the visualization uses AmCharts. AmCharts json is pretty similar to a lot of other charting libraries and may be useful even if you are not using AmCharts.
By setting inject to true, we tell RDS that we want the state's name instead of its code value in the results.

request.description: This query is designed to get the percentage of vote share and chance of winning per candidate in the battle ground states of the 2020 U.S. Presidential Election.

response.name: Battleground State Vote Share and Chance of Winning

url.raw: https://public.richdataservices.com/rds/api/query/us_election_2020/538_presidential_state_toplines_2020/tabulate?dims=date_modeled,us_state_fips&measure=voteshare_incumbent:SUM(voteshare_incumbent),voteshare_challenger:SUM(voteshare_challenger),win_state_incumbent:SUM(win_state_incumbent),win_state_challenger:SUM(win_state_challenger)&where=(us_state_fips=04 OR us_state_fips=13 OR us_state_fips=27 OR us_state_fips=26 OR us_state_fips=12 OR us_state_fips=19 OR us_state_fips=37 OR us_state_fips=39 OR us_state_fips=48 OR us_state_fips=55 OR us_state_fips=42 OR us_state_fips=33)&orderby=date_modeled ASC,us_state_fips ASC&format=amcharts&inject=true

query.description: Our dimensions here are date_modeled and us_state_fips. Dimensions specify how the measures will be grouped when they are returned. In this case, we will have a record with all of our measures per state, per date.

We will be measuring the voteshare and win percentage for the incumbent and challenger. We are summing these values because this is a time series, the default measure would be the count of occurances which will not mean anything. This will be the sum of the voteshare and win percentage by date and state (our dimensions).

query.value: (us_state_fips=04 OR us_state_fips=13 OR us_state_fips=27 OR us_state_fips=26 OR us_state_fips=12 OR us_state_fips=19 OR us_state_fips=37 OR us_state_fips=39 OR us_state_fips=48 OR us_state_fips=55 OR us_state_fips=42 OR us_state_fips=33)
query.description: The where clause here is allowing us to pick out the battleground states rather than all the states. The where clause must use the code value (the FIPS code) rather than the state name.

We are ordering the results by date_modeled from the earliest date to the latest and then by state. The states are coded so the order is by the state code (not name) but in this case it works out alphabetically as well.
We are using the AmCharts format because the visualization uses AmCharts. AmCharts json is pretty similar to a lot of other charting libraries and may be useful even if you are not using AmCharts.
By setting inject to true, we tell RDS that we want the state's name instead of its code value in the results.

item.description:
## Overview State topline data for the U.S. 2020 Presidential elections. Each record measures the chances of winning the state, the chances of winning the Electoral College in the event of a state victory, incumbent margins, and forecasted voteshare per candidate for a state on a particular date that simulations were run. ## Citation Presidential State Toplines 2020 data by dmil at FiveThirtyEight, licensed under CC BY 4.0 ## Provenance

This data product is created through cleaning and simple transformations applied to the Presidential State Toplines 2020 CSV provided by FiveThirtyEight through their github repository. This data is pulled daily.

Data Curation and Transformation

The following data curation and transformation steps have been performed on the source data file.

  • Many variables have been renamed to be more descriptive and unified by topic.
  • The date_modeled variable has been reformatted to be an ISO 8601 date.
  • The model variable has been coded.
  • The state variable has been replaced with us_state_fips which has FIPS codes instead of state names.
  • There were several records whose states were congressional districts, these have been dropped.
  • The variables have be reorganized to have variables about the chance to win the Electoral College based on a state victory (and vice versa) grouped near the chances of state victory.
  • The variables have been reorganized to have the modelling and simulation variables together at the end of the record.

For more in-depth information about the data curation process or how this data product's variables were mapped from the source file, please visit this data product's github repository.

## RDS User Interfaces Want to play with the data before diving into the API? Try out some of our data exploration tools that were built entirely off the API to see what it's capable of. * The data and metadata can be explored using the [RDS Explorer](https://public.richdataservices.com/rds/explorer/explore/us_election_2020/538_presidential_state_toplines_2020/data). * The data can be tabulated using the [RDS TabEngine](https://public.richdataservices.com/rds/tabengine/tabulation/us_election_2020/538_presidential_state_toplines_2020/custom-tables).
